title: Nail Polish on Cotton
stainChemistry: Nail polish contains nitrocellulose (film former), plasticizers (dibutyl phthalate), colorants (pigments/dyes), and solvents (ethyl acetate, butyl acetate). On cotton, the solvents evaporate and the nitrocellulose hardens into a tough, pigmented film that is resistant to water, soap, and most common cleaners. The film bonds mechanically by penetrating fiber texture.
whyThisWorks: Amyl acetate or acetone (where fiber-safe) dissolves the nitrocellulose film on cotton. The solvent reverses the hardening process, re-liquefying the polish so it can be blotted away. Acetone is faster but unsafe on acetate and many synthetics. Amyl acetate is safer for most fibers. The pigment transfers to the blotting cloth as the film dissolves.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply amyl acetate to soften and dissolve the polish film. Blot — do NOT rub
2. Repeat amyl acetate application, blotting dissolved polish onto clean cloth — 3-5 min per pass
3. Apply NSD to remove remaining residue. Flush with cool water
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) for pH restoration
warnings: Apply solvent BEFORE water — water spreads oil stains
NEVER use acetone on acetate fabric — dissolves it on contact

steps: 1. Blot excess polish — do NOT rub or spread
2. Test a hidden area with hydrogen peroxide 3% before proceeding
3. Apply hydrogen peroxide 3% to stain. Blot gently with white cloth
4. Apply Dawn dish soap and work gently. Rinse with warm water
5. Repeat if needed. Air dry — no heat until stain is gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 treatment rounds. Take to a professional dry cleaner — they have specialized solvents for oil-based stains
warnings: Never add water to a fresh oil stain first — water and oil don't mix, you will spread it
Heat from dryer permanently sets oil residue

neverDo: No prolonged acid exposure on colored cotton
No chlorine bleach on colored cotton — strips dyes
fiberSensitivities: Test colorfastness on colored cotton before peroxide

escalation.when: If nail polish stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is nail polish on cotton, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the oil fresh or has it been washed/dried already?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.

homeSolutions.instruction: Test on hidden area first to ensure fabric tolerates acetone-based solutions. DO NOT use this procedure on acetate fabrics — acetone dissolves acetate.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Acetone is volatile and flammable — ensure excellent ventilation and avoid flames.

homeSolutions.instruction: Place fabric stain-side down on glass work surface. Nail polish is a plastic/dry-based stain.
Apply dry-cleaning solvent (lighter fluid) to the stain. Rub gently with the back of a spoon from center outward.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Ensure good ventilation when using dry-cleaning solvent.

homeSolutions.instruction: If polish remains, apply acetone or nail polish remover (test on hidden area first). Rub gently with spoon. Acetone dissolves nail polish effectively.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Acetone is strong and volatile — use in a well-ventilated area. Never use on acetate, rayon, or delicate fabrics.

homeSolutions.instruction: Rinse thoroughly with dry-cleaning solvent to remove acetone residue.
If any residue remains, apply petroleum jelly and rub gently with spoon, then rinse with dry-cleaning solvent.
Allow fabric to air dry completely. If nail polish stain persists or if fabric is acetate or delicate, call a professional cleaner.
